intermediate_messages.content:
1. System Overview
- A backend service that accepts document uploads (PDF, TXT, Markdown), validates type/size, persists original files and metadata, extracts text, segments into searchable units, builds and maintains a searchable index, and supports asynchronous processing with retryable steps. It prevents duplicate records for repeated uploads, supports easy extension to new formats, and provides a status and search API for clients.
- Core goals: maintainability for a small team, clear data model, decoupled components, resilient processing pipeline, and simple yet extensible search capabilities.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities
- API Gateway / REST API layer
  - Input validation, authentication (if any), routing to services
  - Endpoints for upload, status, search, and metadata
  - Output standardized responses
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates file type and size
  - Checks for duplicate uploads (via content hash and/or metadata)
  - Stores original file and metadata in a durable storage
  - Triggers asynchronous processing pipeline
- Document Processing Worker / Orchestrator
  - Orchestrates asynchronous steps: text extraction, cleaning, segmentation, indexing
  - Implements retries with backoff for transient failures
  - Emits processing status events
- Text Extraction Service
  - Extracts raw text from supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown)
  - Returns plain text and basic metadata (pages, headings if available)
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text, removes noise, preserves document structure
  - Splits into searchable sections (e.g., chunks with windowed token limits)
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts a searchable index (per document and per chunk)
  - Uses a simple inverted index or a modular search backend (e.g., lightweight vector/plain inverted index)
  - Stores per-section metadata and vector data if needed
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Stores document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
- Search Service
  - Accepts query terms, returns relevant document sections with scores
  - Supports simple ranking and optional filters (document IDs, date ranges)
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks processing status per document (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status endpoints and dashboards as needed
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes and compares content hashes, checks existing records to avoid duplicates
  - Manages idempotent upload paths

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads a file via API
  - API validates file type/size, computes content hash, checks for duplicates
  - If new, stores original file and metadata, creates a Document record with status “queued”
  - Triggers Processing Workflow (asynchronously)
- Processing Workflow
  - Text Extraction: extract plain text and basic doc-level metadata
  - Text Processing & Segmentation: clean and split into sections/chunks
  - Indexing: create or upsert index entries for each section
  - On success: set Document status to “completed” and store index references
  - On failure: increment retry counter, apply backoff, set status to “failed” after max retries
- Clients can query Status API to learn progress; Search API uses index to return results
- If the same file is uploaded again (duplicate content/hash), return an idempotent response and relevant metadata without reprocessing

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints (high level)
  - POST /uploads
    - Purpose: Upload a new document
    - Main Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, status: "queued" or "duplicate", hash, metadata }
  - GET /uploads/{document_id}/status
    - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
  - GET /documents/{document_id}
    - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
  - GET /search
    - Purpose: Search the index
    - Main Request Data: query parameters: q, limit, offset, filters
    - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
  - GET /health
    - Purpose: Basic health check
    - Main Response Data: { status: "ok" }
- Input/Output conventions
  - Use JSON for responses; use multipart for file uploads
  - Consistent error payloads: { error: { code, message, detail } }

5. Data Model
- Document
  - document_id: UUID
  - hash: string (content hash)
  - mime_type: string
  - size: integer
  - original_path: string (storage reference)
  - metadata: JSON (e.g., title, author, created_date)
  - status: enum (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Section (Indexable Unit)
  - section_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - text: string
  - meta: JSON (e.g., section_index, page_number, offset)
  - vector: optional array (for vector-based search)
  - score_hint: float (ranking hint)
- Index
  - index_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - terms: inverted index mapping (stored as JSON or separate table)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Storage
  - blob_id: UUID
  - path: string
  - mime_type
  - size
  - created_at
- Processing Status / Events
  - event_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - step: string
  - status: enum (pending, success, failed, retried)
  - attempt: integer
  - message: string
  - timestamp

6. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes.go (or equivalent)  // defines endpoints
    - handlers.go              // request handling
  - services/
    - ingestion.go              // upload validation and dedupe trigger
    - orchestration.go          // workflow controller with retries
    - extraction.go             // text extraction interfaces
    - segmentation.go           // chunking logic
    - indexing.go               // index management
    - storage.go                // blob storage interfaces
    - search.go                 // search operations
    - status.go                 // status tracking
  - models/
    - document.go
    - section.go
    - index.go
    - storage.go
    - status.go
  - config/
    - config.go                 // env/configs
  - utils/
    - hash.go                   // content hashing
    - validation.go             // file type/size checks
  - worker/
    - worker.go                 // asynchronous task runner (simple queue abstraction)
- cmd/
  - server/
    - main.go
- docs/
  - design.md

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

8. Testing Plan
- Unit tests
  - Validation logic for file type/size
  - Hash/deduplication decision paths
  - Text extraction interfaces with mock backends for PDF/TXT/Markdown
  - Segmentation rules: section boundaries, token/window size
  - Indexing/upsert logic for sections
- Integration tests
  - End-to-end upload flow for each supported format
  - Duplicate upload flow returns existing document
  - Asynchronous processing path with simulated recoverable errors
- End-to-end tests
  - Upload a sample document, poll status to completion, perform a search over inserted sections
  - Validate search results relevance and snippet content
- Performance tests (light)
  - Large document processing (resource usage, backoff behavior)
- Compatibility tests
  - Easy addition of a new format: add extraction rule stub and verify flow remains intact

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Complexity vs. simplicity in the indexing approach
  - We choose a modular, pluggable indexing design (supporting inverted index or vector-like indexing) to keep the system maintainable for a small team. This allows starting with a simple inverted index (string terms) and incrementally introducing vector-based relevance if needed. The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential future migration work, but gains flexibility to adapt to evolving requirements without a full rewrite.

Component Table
- Component: API Gateway / REST API layer
  - Main Responsibility: Validate inputs, route to services, respond with standardized payloads
  - Input: HTTP requests (multipart/form-data for uploads; JSON for queries)
  - Output: JSON responses with status and data
- Component: Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Main Responsibility: Validate type/size, dedupe, store original, trigger processing
  - Input: Upload payload and metadata
  - Output: Document record creation, processing trigger
- Component: Document Processing Worker / Orchestrator
  - Main Responsibility: Orchestrate asynchronous steps, manage retries, track status
  - Input: Document_id, steps results
  - Output: Updated status, index references
- Component: Text Extraction Service
  - Main Responsibility: Extract raw text and metadata from document formats
  - Input: Document_id, file reference
  - Output: Plain text, document metadata
- Component: Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Main Responsibility: Clean text, segment into sections
  - Input: Plain text
  - Output: List of sections with metadata
- Component: Indexing Service
  - Main Responsibility: Build/upsert index entries for sections
  - Input: Document_id, sections
  - Output: Index references
- Component: Metadata & Storage Service
  - Main Responsibility: Persist metadata, store original file
  - Input: File, metadata
  - Output: Storage pointers, document record
- Component: Search Service
  - Main Responsibility: Execute queries against index, return results
  - Input: Query
  - Output: Matching sections with scores

API Table
- Method: POST
  - Endpoint: /uploads
  - Purpose: Upload a new document
  - Main Request Data: Multipart file, optional metadata
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /uploads/{document_id}/status
  - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
  - Main Request Data: None (path parameter)
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /documents/{document_id}
  - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
  - Main Request Data: None
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /search
  - Purpose: Search index
  - Main Request Data: query q, limit, offset, filters
  - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score }], total }

Note: This design focuses on clarity, maintainability, and incremental extensibility. It avoids committing to a specific external search backend, enabling a small team to implement a simple in-process inverted index now and consider adding vector search later if needed.

Evidence, source needs, constraints, and missing-evidence warnings:

- Evidence gathered from Round 1 planner: System overview, components, workflow, API design, data model, project structure, error handling, testing plan, and a design tradeoff. The design emphasizes (a) asynchronous processing with retries, (b) deduplication via content hash, (c) modular, extensible components, and (d) maintainability for a small team. All items are explicit in the Round 1 content provided by the user.

- Source identifiers: The material to reference is the prompt-provided SE-04 design outline (Round 1 – Planner) which explicitly enumerates:
  - System goals and constraints
  - List of components and responsibilities
  - End-to-end workflow
  - REST API design (endpoints, data formats)
  - Data model (Document, Section, Index, Storage, Status)
  - Suggested project structure
  - Error handling, retries, and duplicate prevention
  - Testing plan
  - Design tradeoff
  - Component Table and API Table

- Constraints to respect from prompt:
  - Files: PDF, TXT, Markdown only; validate type and size
  - Persist original file + metadata
  - Text extraction, segmentation into searchable sections
  - Build a searchable index; support document search
  - Asynchronous processing with retryable steps
  - Prevent duplicates for repeated uploads
  - Easy addition of new formats
  - Maintainable for small team
  - REST API with status and search capabilities
  - Approximate design length: 700–900 words
  - Include: System Overview; Main Components; End-to-End Workflow; REST API; Data Model; Project Structure; Error/Retry/Duplicate; Testing Plan; Design Tradeoff; and two tables: Component Table and API Table
  - Provide a design, not a runnable app; short pseudocode or interfaces allowed
  - Use the specified Markdown sections format
  - Do not cite external sources

- Missing-evidence warnings (areas not requiring empirical evidence but worth clarifying in implementation):
  - Specific choice of underlying storage technologies (e.g., S3-like blob store vs. local disk) is not prescribed; design remains abstract to allow simple substitution.
  - The exact search backend (inverted index vs. vector search) is left pluggable; initial design suggests inverted index with potential vector extension.
  - Authentication/authorization are mentioned as optional in outline but not specified in detail; current design assumes an API layer with potential placeholders for auth.
  - Performance characteristics (throughput, latency) are not quantified; the design is qualitative with backoff/backpressure considerations.
  - Commit/consistency model (strong vs. eventual) is described as eventual for indexing; concrete consistency guarantees are left to implementation.

- If this needs to be expanded with concrete artifacts for handoff, consider providing:
  - Sequence diagrams for upload -> processing -> search
  - Concrete interface definitions (e.g., Go interfaces, Python ABCs, or TypeScript types) for the extraction, segmentation, and indexing modules
  - Example JSON payloads for key API responses
  - VCS-oriented project scaffolding (GitHub Actions workflow names, test commands)

- Suggested next steps to fulfill potential gaps:
  - Decide on a primary storage backend (e.g., object store + relational DB) and document that as a separate architecture decision.
  - Add a lightweight message queue abstraction (in-process or external like Rabbit/Kafka) with retry policies.
  - Define concrete idempotency keys and dedupe strategy across uploads, including handling of partial failures.
  - Draft concrete health-check and metrics collection plan (latency, queue depth, error rate).

1. System Overview
The design describes a backend service pair that accepts PDF, TXT, and Markdown uploads, validates type and size, stores the original file and metadata, extracts text, splits into searchable sections, builds an index, and supports document search. Processing is asynchronous with retryable steps, and idempotent handling prevents duplicate records for repeated uploads. The architecture emphasizes modular, pluggable components to ease maintenance for a small team and enable easy addition of new formats.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities
- API Gateway / REST API Layer
  - Input validation, routing, and standardized responses.
  - Endpoints for upload, status, document metadata, and search.
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates mime type and size; computes content hash.
  - Checks for duplicates; persists original file and metadata.
  - Triggers asynchronous processing workflow.
- Document Processing Orchestrator
  - Coordinates asynchronous steps: extraction, processing, indexing.
  - Implements retries with backoff; emits processing status events.
- Text Extraction Service
  - Extracts plain text and basic metadata from supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown).
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text; segments into searchable sections (chunks with boundaries).
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts index entries per section; stores metadata and optional vectors.
  - Supports pluggable backends (inverted index now; vector search later).
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Persists document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash).
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage.
- Search Service
  - Executes queries against the index; returns relevant sections with scores and snippets.
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks per-document status (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried).
  - Exposes status endpoints and lightweight dashboards.
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes content hash; checks existing records to ensure idempotent uploads.

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads a file via POST /uploads with multipart/form-data (file + optional metadata).
  - API validates type/size; computes content hash; checks for duplicates.
  - If new, stores original file and metadata; creates a Document record with status “queued.”
  - Triggers asynchronous Processing Workflow.
- Processing Workflow (orchestrator)
  - Text Extraction: obtain plain text and basic doc metadata.
  - Text Processing & Segmentation: clean, normalize, and split into sections.
  - Indexing: upsert index entries for each section.
  - On success: mark document as “completed” and store index references.
  - On failure: increment retry count; apply backoff; mark as “failed” after max retries.
- Clients poll GET /uploads/{document_id}/status for progress.
- Search via GET /search queries the index and returns matching sections with scores.
- Duplicate uploads: if the same content hash is detected, return an idempotent response referencing existing document_id without reprocessing.

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints
  - POST /uploads
    - Purpose: Upload a new document
    - Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
    - Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
  - GET /uploads/{document_id}/status
    - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
    - Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
  - GET /documents/{document_id}
    - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
    - Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
  - GET /search
    - Purpose: Execute a search query against the index
    - Request Data: q, limit, offset, filters
    - Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
  - GET /health
    - Purpose: Basic health check
    - Response Data: { status: "ok" }
- Data Formats
  - JSON for responses; multipart/form-data for uploads
  - Consistent error payloads: { error: { code, message, detail } }

5. Data Model
- Document
  - document_id: UUID
  - hash: string
  - mime_type: string
  - size: integer
  - original_path: string
  - metadata: JSON (title, author, created_date)
  - status: enum (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Section (Indexable Unit)
  - section_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - text: string
  - meta: JSON (section_index, page_number, offset)
  - vector: optional array
  - score_hint: float
- Index
  - index_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - terms: inverted index mapping (JSON or normalized tables)
  - created_at / updated_at
- Storage
  - blob_id: UUID
  - path: string
  - mime_type
  - size
  - created_at
- Processing Status / Events
  - event_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - step: string
  - status: enum (pending, success, failed, retried)
  - attempt: integer
  - message: string
  - timestamp

6. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes.go
    - handlers.go
  - services/
    - ingestion.go
    - orchestration.go
    - extraction.go
    - segmentation.go
    - indexing.go
    - storage.go
    - search.go
    - status.go
  - models/
    - document.go
    - section.go
    - index.go
    - storage.go
    - status.go
  - config/
    - config.go
  - utils/
    - hash.go
    - validation.go
  - worker/
    - worker.go
- cmd/
  - server/
    - main.go
- docs/
  - design.md

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: on upload, compute content hash; if exists, return existing document_id with status; no reprocessing. Idempotent upload path.
- Retries: processing steps are idempotent with exponential backoff up to max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors retried; permanent errors mark as failed.
- Observability: per-document logs, status transitions, and error messages stored; endpoints to fetch failures.
- Transactional boundaries: original storage and metadata writes succeed before processing; index writes may be eventual to decouple from ingestion.

8. Testing Plan
- Unit tests
  - File type/size validation
  - Hashing and deduplication logic
  - Extraction/segmentation interfaces (mock backends for formats)
  - Segmentation rules and boundary handling
  - Indexing/upsert logic
- Integration tests
  - End-to-end upload for each format
  - Duplicate upload path returns existing document_id
  - Processing path with simulated transient errors
- End-to-end tests
  - Upload, poll status to completion, then perform search and verify results/snippets
  - Reuse of document across searches to confirm index linkage
- Performance tests (light)
  - Processing larger documents; observe backoff behavior
- Extendability tests
  - Adding a new format should require minimal changes to extraction/segmentation adapters

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Indexing approach complexity vs maintainability
  - Build a modular, pluggable indexing layer that can start with a simple inverted index and later incorporate vector-based search. This preserves a small team's simplicity now, while providing a clear path to sophistication. The tradeoff is an initial abstraction layer and potential migration work in the future, but it avoids locking into a single backend and supports gradual enhancement.

Component Table
- API Gateway / REST API Layer
  - Main Responsibility: Validate inputs, route to services, standardized responses
  - Input: HTTP requests
  - Output: JSON responses
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Main Responsibility: Validate, dedupe, store original, trigger processing
  - Input: Upload payload and metadata
  - Output: Document record, processing trigger
- Document Processing Orchestrator
  - Main Responsibility: Manage asynchronous steps and retries; status
  - Input: document_id
  - Output: Updated status and index references
- Text Extraction Service
  - Main Responsibility: Extract plain text and basic metadata
  - Input: document reference
  - Output: plain text, doc metadata
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Main Responsibility: Clean and chunk text into sections
  - Input: plain text
  - Output: sections with metadata
- Indexing Service
  - Main Responsibility: Build/upsert index entries
  - Input: document_id, sections
  - Output: index references
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Main Responsibility: Persist metadata and store original file
  - Input: file, metadata
  - Output: storage pointers, document record
- Search Service
  - Main Responsibility: Execute queries and return results
  - Input: query
  - Output: results with snippets and scores

API Table
- Method: POST
  - Endpoint: /uploads
  - Purpose: Upload a new document
  - Main Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /uploads/{document_id}/status
  - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
  - Main Request Data: path parameter
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /documents/{document_id}
  - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
  - Main Request Data: path parameter
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /search
  - Purpose: Perform a search
  - Main Request Data: q, limit, offset, filters
  - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score }], total }

Note: The design remains implementation-agnostic, focusing on clear responsibilities, maintainability, and incremental extensibility.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Post verification findings, coordination risks, and required fixes. Read the current board and avoid duplicating resolved material.
intermediate_messages.content:
Verification findings and required fixes

1) Constraints alignment
- File support: PDF, TXT, Markdown explicitly required. The design mentions these formats consistently in overview and workflow, with validation of mime types and size. Status: satisfied.
- Original + metadata persistence: Covered by Metadata & Storage Service; Document model includes hash, storage path, and metadata. Status: satisfied.
- Text extraction, segmentation, indexing: Present via Extraction, Segmentation, Indexing services; end-to-end workflow described. Status: satisfied.
- Asynchronous processing with retryable steps: Orchestrator with retries/backoff described. Status: satisfied.
- Duplicate prevention: Hash-based dedupe and idempotent upload path described. Status: satisfied.
- Easy addition of new formats: Pluggable extraction/segmentation adapters and modular design. Status: satisfied.
- Maintainability for small team: Modular components, suggested project structure, mockable interfaces. Status: satisfied.
- REST API for status and search: Endpoints provided (uploads, status, documents, search). Status: satisfied.
- Length of design: Approximately 700–900 words? The current design is detailed and may exceed target word count. Action: tighten if needed for final handoff, but within the requested range (700-900) it is borderline but acceptable since there are multiple sections; ensure final edit stays within range.

2) Completeness of required sections
- System Overview: Present.
- Main Components and Responsibilities: Present (Component Table included later).
- End-to-End Workflow: Present.
- REST API Design: Present (Endpoints listed).
- Data Model: Present (Document, Section, Index, Storage, Status).
- Suggested Project Structure: Present.
- Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention: Present.
- Testing Plan: Present.
- Design Tradeoff: Present.
- Component Table: Present (described in narrative; labeled table blocks included).
- API Table: Present (labeled blocks included).

3) Potential inconsistencies or gaps
- Explicit data stores: The design remains abstract about the concrete storage tech (object store vs local disk) and the database choice. This is acceptable for a design document but should be materialized in a concrete architecture decision later.
- Status progression semantics: Terms used (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried) are defined, but progress metrics (e.g., percentage or step-level progress) are only alluded to. Consider adding a progress grain (step-level vs. %).
- Idempotency keys: The design uses content hash dedupe but does not specify handling of partial uploads or different metadata but same content. Clarify: for duplicates, return existing document_id; for different metadata with identical content, define whether to treat as duplicate or allow separate records with an idempotency key policy.
- Consistency model: Indexing is described as eventual; ensure downstream search correctness in absence of immediate index writes. Could add a brief note on consistency guarantees and whether read-after-write is allowed.
- Testing plan scope: Adequate; consider adding contract tests for the API layer and schema migrations, and CI scaffolding suggestions.

4) Coordination risks
- Scope creep on formats: If new formats are added, extraction/segmentation may require significant work. Mitigation: keep format adapters isolated behind interfaces with clear versioning.
- Backpressure and queueing: The design describes an in-process or pluggable queue. If real queues are not chosen early, testing async behavior could be brittle. Mitigation: specify a minimal queue abstraction early and define metrics (queue depth, average processing time).
- Data model evolution: Adding fields (e.g., more complex metadata or new indexing features) should be documented with migration plans to prevent churn. Mitigation: add a lightweight migration strategy in docs.

5) Required fixes and clarifications to proceed
- Add concrete interface definitions (e.g., for extraction, segmentation, indexing) to enable team to implement adapters consistently. Provide at least pseudocode or TypeScript/Go-like interfaces.
- Include example JSON payloads for typical API responses and error formats for consistency.
- Specify a lightweight queue abstraction in code structure (e.g., worker.go) and outline how tasks are serialized and persisted for idempotency.
- Provide a brief health-check and metrics plan (e.g., /healthz, /metrics) and how they integrate with the status service.
- Tighten the API surface with explicit error payload structure, including error codes and internal vs. client-visible details.
- Ensure the design word count remains within 700–900 words for your target. If needed, trim the narrative while preserving essential elements and the Component/API Tables.

Recommended next steps
- Add concrete interface sketches for extraction, segmentation, and indexing modules.
- Include example payloads (uploads response, status, search result) to aid implementation.
- Draft a short sequence diagram outline (upload -> ingestion -> processing -> search) for quick reference.
- Decide on a primary storage/DB stack and document as an Architecture Decision Record (ADR).

This critique confirms that the design largely satisfies the prompt constraints with a coherent, maintainable architecture. The main actions are to add implementation-ready interface definitions, concrete error payloads, and a concise word-count edit if strict length is required.

intermediate_messages.content:
System Overview
- A backend service for uploading PDFs, TXTs, and Markdown files, validating type/size, persisting original files and metadata, extracting text, segmenting into searchable sections, building a retrievable index, and enabling document search. Processing is asynchronous with retryable steps and idempotent deduplication to avoid duplicates. The design favors modular, pluggable components to ease maintenance for a small team and supports easy addition of new formats.

1. Main Components and Responsibilities
- API Gateway / REST API Layer
  - Input validation, routing, standardized responses
  - Endpoints: uploads, status, documents, search, health
  - Input: HTTP requests (multipart/form-data for uploads; JSON for searches/status)
  - Output: JSON payloads; appropriate HTTP status codes
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates mime type/size; computes content hash
  - Checks for duplicates; persists original file + metadata
  - Triggers asynchronous processing workflow
  - Input: file, optional metadata
  - Output: document_id, initial status
- Document Processing Orchestrator
  - Orchestrates asynchronous steps: extraction, processing, indexing
  - Retries with backoff; emits status events
  - Input: document_id
  - Output: updated status, references to index/sections
- Text Extraction Service
  - Extracts plain text and basic metadata from supported formats
  - Input: document reference
  - Output: plain_text, doc_metadata
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text; segments into searchable sections (chunks with boundaries)
  - Input: plain_text
  - Output: sections[] with metadata
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts index entries for each section
  - Stores per-section metadata and optional vectors
  - Input: document_id, sections
  - Output: index references
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Persists document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
  - Input: file, metadata
  - Output: storage pointers, document record
- Search Service
  - Executes queries against the index; returns relevant sections with scores and snippets
  - Input: query, optional filters
  - Output: results[] with document_id, snippet, score, metadata
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks per-document status (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status data via API; supports lightweight dashboards
  - Input: document_id, step results
  - Output: status records
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes content hash; checks existing docs; ensures idempotent uploads
  - Input: file content
  - Output: dedupe decision (duplicate vs new)

2. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads via POST /uploads (multipart file + metadata)
  - Validation (type/size), compute hash, check duplicates
  - If new: store original + metadata; create Document with status “queued”; trigger processing
  - If duplicate: return existing document_id with current status (idempotent path)
- Processing Workflow (async)
  - Text Extraction -> Text Processing & Segmentation -> Indexing
  - On success: mark document as “completed”; store index references
  - On failure: retry with exponential backoff up to max_retries; on final failure mark “failed”
- Clients can poll GET /uploads/{document_id}/status for progress
- Search via GET /search on completed indices; returns top matches with snippets
- Duplicate handling: hash-based dedupe ensures identical content does not create new records

3. REST API Design
- Endpoints
  - POST /uploads
    - Purpose: Upload a new document
    - Request Data: multipart/form-data (file, optional metadata)
    - Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
  - GET /uploads/{document_id}/status
    - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
    - Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
  - GET /documents/{document_id}
    - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
    - Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
  - GET /search
    - Purpose: Execute a search query
    - Request Data: q, limit, offset, filters
    - Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
  - GET /health
    - Purpose: Health check
    - Response Data: { status: "ok" }
- Data Formats
  - Responses as JSON; uploads as multipart/form-data
  - Error payloads: { error: { code, message, detail } }

4. Data Model
- Document
  - document_id: UUID
  - hash: string
  - mime_type: string
  - size: integer
  - original_path: string
  - metadata: JSON (title, author, created_date, etc.)
  - status: enum (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - created_at, updated_at
- Section (Indexable Unit)
  - section_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - text: string
  - meta: JSON (section_index, page_number, offset)
  - vector: optional array
  - score_hint: float
- Index
  - index_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - terms: inverted index mapping (JSON or relational tables)
  - created_at, updated_at
- Storage
  - blob_id: UUID
  - path: string
  - mime_type, size
  - created_at
- Processing Status / Events
  - event_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - step: string
  - status: enum (pending, success, failed, retried)
  - attempt: integer
  - message: string
  - timestamp

5. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes.go
    - handlers.go
  - services/
    - ingestion.go
    - orchestration.go
    - extraction.go
    - segmentation.go
    - indexing.go
    - storage.go
    - search.go
    - status.go
  - models/
    - document.go
    - section.go
    - index.go
    - storage.go
    - status.go
  - config/
    - config.go
  - utils/
    - hash.go
    - validation.go
  - worker/
    - worker.go
- cmd/
  - server/
    - main.go
- docs/
  - design.md

6. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types or oversized files with 400 and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: on upload, compute content hash; if exists, respond with existing document_id (idempotent path); no reprocessing.
- Retries: processing steps are idempotent; retry with exponential backoff up to max_attempts (e.g., 5); transient errors retried; permanent errors mark as failed.
- Observability: per-document logs, status transitions, error messages; endpoints to fetch failures
- Consistency: storage/metadata writes precede processing; indexing may be eventual to decouple ingestion

7. Testing Plan
- Unit tests
  - Validation, hash/dedupe logic, extraction/segmentation adapters, indexing upsert
- Integration tests
  - End-to-end upload per format
  - Duplicate upload path returns existing document_id
  - Processing path with simulated transient errors
- End-to-end tests
  - Upload, poll status to completion, search results/snippet validation
- Performance tests (light)
  - Large document processing and backoff behavior
- Extendability tests
  - Adding a new format via adapter stubs

8. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Indexing simplicity vs extensibility
  - Start with a pluggable indexing layer allowing an inverted index now and vector search later. Gains maintainability and incremental evolution for a small team at the cost of initial abstraction and possible migration work later.

Component Table
- Component: API Gateway / REST API Layer
  - Main Responsibility: Validate inputs, route to services, standardized responses
  - Input: HTTP requests
  - Output: JSON responses
- Component: Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Main Responsibility: Validate, dedupe, store original, trigger processing
  - Input: Upload payload and metadata
  - Output: Document record, processing trigger
- Component: Document Processing Orchestrator
  - Main Responsibility: Manage async steps and retries; status
  - Input: document_id
  - Output: Updated status and index references
- Component: Text Extraction Service
  - Main Responsibility: Extract plain text and basic metadata
  - Input: document reference
  - Output: plain text, doc metadata
- Component: Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Main Responsibility: Clean and chunk text
  - Input: plain text
  - Output: sections with metadata
- Component: Indexing Service
  - Main Responsibility: Build/upsert index entries
  - Input: document_id, sections
  - Output: index references
- Component: Metadata & Storage Service
  - Main Responsibility: Persist metadata and store original file
  - Input: file, metadata
  - Output: storage pointers, document record
- Component: Search Service
  - Main Responsibility: Execute queries and return results
  - Input: query
  - Output: results with snippets and scores

API Table
- Method: POST
  - Endpoint: /uploads
  - Purpose: Upload a new document
  - Main Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /uploads/{document_id}/status
  - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
  - Main Request Data: path parameter
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /documents/{document_id}
  - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
  - Main Request Data: path parameter
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /search
  - Purpose: Execute a search
  - Main Request Data: q, limit, offset, filters
  - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /health
  - Purpose: Health check
  - Main Request Data: None
  - Main Response Data: { status: "ok" }

Notes
- This is a design skeleton intended for handoff and discussion. It remains implementation-agnostic and focuses on clear responsibilities, extensibility, and maintainability for a small team. Concrete interface definitions, example payloads, and a lightweight queue abstraction should be added in a follow-up to enable implementation.

  - Supported file types: PDF, TXT, Markdown

Source needs and what to clarify next
- Concrete technology choices are deliberately left abstract (storage backend, DB, search backend, message queue). Decision needs to be documented later (ADR) to avoid drift:
  - Storage: object store vs. local blob storage; metadata DB choice
  - Indexing backend: start with inverted index; plans to add vector search

- Interface definitions for core adapters are not yet explicit:
  - Text Extraction interface for PDF/TXT/Markdown adapters

  - Indexing/upsert interface (inverted index schema, update semantics)

- Authentication/authorization: optional in prompt; current design assumes an API layer but does not specify auth, tenant scoping, or access controls.
- Health/metrics: high-level /health endpoint exists; consider metrics endpoints (/metrics) and health probes (liveness/readiness) for deployment environments.
- Idempotency edge cases:
  - If uploads include identical content but differing metadata, decision policy is not fully specified (dedupe by content hash would treat as duplicate; metadata variance may require separate records). Clarify idempotency rules for metadata changes with identical content.
- Performance and SLA targets: no quantified goals (throughput, latency, max file size, max concurrent processing); define targets for validation in testing plan.
- Testing details:
  - While unit/integration/end-to-end tests are described, concrete test data sets (sample documents), mocks for extraction/segmentation/indexing, and CI workflows are not specified.

Suggestions for immediate artifacts to produce

  - Storage choice and rationale
  - Indexing strategy (inverted vs vector; future pluggability)

- Interface sketches for adapters:
  - ExtractionAdapter with methods extract(document_id, file_ref) -> (text, metadata)
  - SegmentationAdapter with methods segment(text) -> []Section

Constraints to monitor during design progression

intermediate_messages.content:
System analysis and grounded conclusions

Executive summary
The SE-04 design provides a solid, modular backend architecture for a document-processing and search service supporting PDF, TXT, and Markdown uploads. It emphasizes asynchronous processing, retryable steps, duplicate avoidance via content hashing, and easy extensibility for new formats. The proposal remains implementation-agnostic, which is appropriate for a small team but requires concrete interface definitions, data-store decisions, and clear API error semantics to move toward implementation.

Key recalculations and constraints check
- File formats: Explicitly supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown) are consistently enforced in validation and workflow. No changes needed.
- File validation: Size/type checks are explicit; error handling for invalid inputs is defined (400 with descriptive payloads). No changes needed.
- Deduplication: Content-hash-based dedupe path; duplicates return existing document_id with current status. This is idempotent for identical uploads. A potential edge case (metadata differences with identical content) should be clarified in an ADR, but the current design supports the primary use case.
- Asynchronous processing: Orchestrator with retries and exponential backoff (up to a max, e.g., 5 attempts) is specified. This aligns with resilience goals; ensure backoff policy is codified in the implementation to avoid thundering retries.
- Indexing approach: Start with a pluggable/inverted-index-based design, with vector search as a future enhancement. This balances simplicity now with extensibility later. Do not lock into a single backend; keep an abstraction layer.
- Data model and workflow: Document, Section, Index, Storage, and Status/Events models align with the workflow: ingest, extract, segment, index, search. End-to-end steps map cleanly to services.
- Testing: Coverage spans unit, integration, and end-to-end tests, including duplicate upload scenarios and transient-error handling. Ensure test data includes all three formats and representative edge cases.
- Project structure: Modular components and a shallow, maintainable codebase are provided. This supports a small team and future feature adds.

Grounded conclusions by constraint

1) System overview fidelity
- The architecture meets core objectives: support for PDF/TXT/Markdown, validation, persistence of originals and metadata, text extraction, segmentation, indexing, search, asynchronous processing, retries, and duplicate prevention. It also accommodates growth (new formats, different storage/search backends) with a modular design.

2) Main components viability
- The component decomposition covers all lifecycle stages: ingestion, orchestration, extraction, segmentation, indexing, storage, search, and status/events. This separation supports independent testing and straightforward maintenance.

3) End-to-end workflow correctness
- The end-to-end sequence (upload -> validate hash -> dedupe check -> store original/metadata -> enqueue -> extract -> segment -> index -> status completed or failed with retries) is coherent and implementable. Duplicates are handled idempotently; status can be queried; search uses the index to produce results.

4) REST API design practicality
- Endpoints (/uploads, /uploads/{id}/status, /documents/{id}, /search, /health) cover the required interactions. Consistent response shapes and error payloads are defined. For production, consider expanding with /healthz and /metrics endpoints and possibly versioning.

5) Data model adequacy
- Document, Section, Index, Storage, and Processing Status entities capture necessary attributes to support dedupe, indexing, search, and observability. The separation of Storage and Original File references aligns with durability and retrievability goals.

6) Project structure maintainability
- The proposed src layout (api, services, models, worker) supports incremental development, easy testing, and clear ownership. Interfaces for adapters (extraction, segmentation, indexing) can be defined to enforce consistency.

7) Error handling, retry, and duplicates
- Validation errors -> 400 with explicit messages.
- Dedupe path ensures idempotent behavior on duplicates; consider metadata-difference edge cases in ADRs.
- Retries use exponential backoff; steps are designed to be idempotent to avoid side effects on retries.
- Observability is addressed via per-document status/event records; consider integrating metrics for queue depth and processing latency.

8) Testing strategy sufficiency
- The plan covers unit, integration, and end-to-end testing, including large documents and failure/retry scenarios. For practical adoption, define concrete test datasets, mock adapters for extraction/segmentation/indexing, and CI pipelines with deterministic run orders.

9) Design tradeoff interpretation
- Tradeoff chosen: modular, pluggable indexing (inverted index now, vector search later) to maximize maintainability and incremental enhancement for a small team. Acceptable complexity overhead for the flexibility gained; ensure clean abstraction boundaries to minimize migration pain later.

Design gaps to address before implementation
- Concrete adapter interfaces: Provide explicit interfaces for TextExtraction, Segmentation, and Indexing (e.g., in Go or TypeScript) with method signatures and expected return types.
- Architecture Decision Records (ADR): Document storage backend choice, indexing backend selection, and queueing strategy (in-process vs external, retry behavior).
- Error payload schema: Standardize error codes, messages, and internal vs. user-facing details; provide example JSON payloads.
- Idempotency policy details: Clarify behavior when identical content arrives with different metadata; decide if metadata should trigger a separate record or be treated as duplicate.
- Metrics and health: Add /healthz and /metrics conventions; define which metrics to emit (latency, queue depth, success/failure rates).

Suggested concrete artifacts to create next
- Type/Interface sketches for adapters (ExtractionAdapter, SegmentationAdapter, IndexingAdapter) with example signatures.
- ADR document outlining storage, indexing backend, queueing approach, and consistency guarantees.
- Example payloads: sample Upload response, Status response, Search results, and a structured error example.
- Lightweight sequence diagram outline (upload -> ingestion -> processing -> search) for quick reference.

Bottom line
The SE-04 design is sound for a small team: clear responsibilities, robust asynchronous flow with retries, and sensible extensibility. Proceeding with concrete adapter interfaces, ADRs, and example payloads will unlock implementation while preserving the intended maintainability and future-proofing.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Post verification findings, coordination risks, and required fixes. Read the current board and avoid duplicating resolved material.
intermediate_messages.content:
Coordination findings and required fixes for SE-04 design

1) Compliance verification (constraints satisfied)
- Supported formats: PDF, TXT, Markdown consistently enforced in overview, workflow, and data model. Deduplication by content hash present. Asynchronous processing and retry/backoff described. Duplicate prevention clearly addressed. Extensibility for new formats via pluggable adapters. REST API surfaces cover upload, status, document metadata, and search. Overall alignment with prompt.

2) Specific gaps or ambiguities to address
- Idempotency edge cases with metadata variance: The design relies on content hash to dedupe. Ambiguity remains when identical content arrives with differing metadata. Action: add an ADR clarifying policy for metadata-difference cases (e.g., treat as duplicate and reuse existing document_id, or allow a new document with an idempotency key). Ensure explicit decision and follow-up code contract.
- Consistency model for indexing: Index writes are described as eventual; potential read-after-write concerns during short-lived processing. Action: specify consistency guarantees (e.g., search results are consistent for completed documents; reads during processing may return partial results). Consider a readiness check to gate search exposure until a document is completed.
- Concrete adapter interfaces missing: No explicit interface definitions for TextExtraction, Segmentation, and Indexing adapters (method signatures, error handling, data shapes). Action: add language-specific interfaces (Go, TS, or Python) with clear input/output contracts to enable parallel implementation and testing.
- Error payload standardization: Generic error payloads are described, but concrete schema examples (error codes, messages, details, HTTP status mapping) are absent. Action: define a standard error schema, including codes (e.g., INVALID_TYPE, TOO_LARGE, DUPLICATE_DETECTED, PROCESSING_ERROR), and provide JSON examples.
- Queueing/async backbone: A “document Processing Orchestrator” and a worker/queue concept exist, but no concrete queue abstraction or persistence guarantees. Action: specify a minimal queue abstraction (in-process or external) with serialization, idempotency keys, and at-least-once delivery semantics; document how retries are coordinated across steps.
- Exposure of health and metrics: /health exists; consider additional health probes (/healthz, /readiness) and metrics endpoint to observe queue depth, processing latencies, and error rates. Action: add ADRs and example metrics payloads or exporters.
- Metadata vs storage coupling: Original storage reference and metadata fields are described, but explicit storage backend choices (object store vs local disk, blob semantics) are deferred. Action: capture a concrete ADR for storage backend and schema evolution strategies.
- Testing plan depth: Coverage is broad but lacks concrete test data, mocks, and CI scaffolding. Action: define representative test documents (PDF/TXT/Markdown), mock adapters, and a minimal CI test plan with commands and environment assumptions.
- Edge-case handling for partial failures: Partial processing (e.g., extraction succeeds but segmentation fails) needs explicit rollback/compensation semantics. Action: codify step-level idempotency and compensating actions, and ensure status transitions reflect partial failures clearly.

3) Potential design improvements (design-level)
- Architecture Decision Records (ADR) needed: ADRs for storage, indexing backend, queueing strategy, and consistency guarantees to avoid drift.
- Adapter interface sketches: Provide concrete interfaces (e.g., ExtractionAdapter, SegmentationAdapter, IndexingAdapter) with sample signatures and example data types.
- Example payloads: Supply concrete JSON examples for successful/duplicate uploads, status responses, search results, and error responses to accelerate implementation.
- Edge-case policy document: Clarify dedupe semantics when metadata changes with identical content; align with business rules.

4) Coordination risks to mitigate
- Format-specific work: Adding formats later may require significant adapters; mitigate with strict interface contracts and a small, well-scoped adapter per format.
- Async behavior verification: Without a defined queue/backoff policy, tests may be brittle. Mitigate by introducing a minimal, deterministic queue abstraction and test harness.
- Data migration risk: Evolving data model (new fields, indexing features) could disrupt existing records. Mitigate with versioned schemas and clear migration steps in ADRs.
- Performance targets: No quantified SLAs; mitigate by adding target metrics in ADRs and basic load tests in the testing plan.

5) Immediate deliverables to finalize before implementation
- Explicit adapter interfaces (text extraction, segmentation, indexing) with method signatures and data types.
- ADRs documenting:
  - Storage backend choice and rationale
  - Indexing strategy (inverted index now, vector search later) and pluggability
  - Queueing approach and retry semantics
  - Consistency and read-after-write guarantees
- Concrete error payload schema with examples:
  - Error codes, user-facing vs internal details, HTTP status mapping
- Idempotency policy document for metadata-differing duplicates
- Example payloads:
  - Upload response, Status response, Document metadata, Search results, and Error examples
- Lightweight sequence diagram outline for upload -> ingestion -> processing -> search

Conclusion
The SE-04 design is solid for maintainability and incremental extensibility but requires concrete adapter interfaces, explicit ADRs, standardized error schemas, and clarified edge-case policies to move toward implementation with confidence.

1. System Overview
- A backend service that accepts document uploads (PDF, TXT, Markdown), validates type/size, persists original files and metadata, extracts text, segments into searchable units, builds and maintains a searchable index, and supports asynchronous processing with retryable steps. It prevents duplicate records for repeated uploads, supports easy extension to new formats, and provides a status and search API for clients.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities

- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates file type and size
  - Checks for duplicate uploads (via content hash and/or metadata)
  - Stores original file and metadata in a durable storage
  - Triggers asynchronous processing pipeline
- Document Processing Worker / Orchestrator
  - Orchestrates asynchronous steps: text extraction, cleaning, segmentation, indexing
  - Implements retries with backoff for transient failures
  - Emits processing status events

  - Stores document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
- Search Service
  - Accepts query terms, returns relevant document sections with scores
  - Supports simple ranking and optional filters (document IDs, date ranges)
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks processing status per document (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status endpoints and dashboards as needed

  - Computes and compares content hashes, checks existing records to avoid duplicates

  - Returns plain text and basic metadata (pages, headings if available)
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text, removes noise, preserves document structure
  - Splits into searchable sections (e.g., chunks with windowed token limits)
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts a searchable index (per document and per chunk)
  - Uses a simple inverted index or a modular search backend (e.g., lightweight vector/plain inverted index)
  - Stores per-section metadata and vector data if needed
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Stores document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
- Search Service
  - Accepts query terms, returns relevant document sections with scores
  - Supports simple ranking and optional filters (document IDs, date ranges)
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks processing status per document (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status endpoints and dashboards as needed
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes and compares content hashes, checks existing records to avoid duplicates
  - Manages idempotent upload paths

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads a file via API
  - API validates file type/size, computes content hash, checks for duplicates
  - If new, stores original file and metadata, creates a Document record with status “queued”
  - Triggers Processing Workflow (asynchronously)
- Processing Workflow
  - Text Extraction: extract plain text and basic doc-level metadata
  - Text Processing & Segmentation: clean and split into sections/chunks
  - Indexing: create or upsert index entries for each section
  - On success: set Document status to “completed” and store index references
  - On failure: increment retry counter, apply backoff, set status to “failed” after max retries
- Clients can query Status API to learn progress; Search API uses index to return results
- If the same file is uploaded again (duplicate content/hash), return an idempotent response and relevant metadata without reprocessing

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints (high level)
  - POST /uploads
    - Purpose: Upload a new document
    - Main Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, status: "queued" or "duplicate", hash, metadata }
  - GET /uploads/{document_id}/status
    - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
  - GET /documents/{document_id}
    - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
  - GET /search

    - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
  - GET /health

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

- Compatibility tests

The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential future migration work, but gains flexibility to adapt to evolving requirements without a full rewrite.

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

- Tradeoff: Complexity vs. simplicity in the indexing approach

This allows starting with a simple inverted index (string terms) and incrementally introducing vector-based relevance if needed. The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential future migration work, but gains flexibility to adapt to evolving requirements without a full rewrite.

1. System Overview
- A backend service that accepts document uploads (PDF, TXT, Markdown), validates type/size, persists original files and metadata, extracts text, segments into searchable units, builds and maintains a searchable index, and supports asynchronous processing with retryable steps. It prevents duplicate records for repeated uploads, supports easy extension to new formats, and provides a status and search API for clients.
- Core goals: maintainability for a small team, clear data model, decoupled components, resilient processing pipeline, and simple yet extensible search capabilities.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities
- API Gateway / REST API layer
  - Input validation, authentication (if any), routing to services
  - Endpoints for upload, status, search, and metadata
  - Output standardized responses
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates file type and size
  - Checks for duplicate uploads (via content hash and/or metadata)
  - Stores original file and metadata in a durable storage
  - Triggers asynchronous processing pipeline
- Document Processing Worker / Orchestrator
  - Orchestrates asynchronous steps: text extraction, cleaning, segmentation, indexing
  - Implements retries with backoff for transient failures
  - Emits processing status events
- Text Extraction Service
  - Extracts raw text from supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown)
  - Returns plain text and basic metadata (pages, headings if available)
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text, removes noise, preserves document structure
  - Splits into searchable sections (e.g., chunks with windowed token limits)
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts a searchable index (per document and per chunk)
  - Uses a simple inverted index or a modular search backend (e.g., lightweight vector/plain inverted index)
  - Stores per-section metadata and vector data if needed
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Stores document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
- Search Service
  - Accepts query terms, returns relevant document sections with scores
  - Supports simple ranking and optional filters (document IDs, date ranges)
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks processing status per document (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status endpoints and dashboards as needed
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes and compares content hashes, checks existing records to avoid duplicates
  - Manages idempotent upload paths

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads a file via API
  - API validates file type/size, computes content hash, checks for duplicates
  - If new, stores original file and metadata, creates a Document record with status “queued”
  - Triggers Processing Workflow (asynchronously)
- Processing Workflow
  - Text Extraction: extract plain text and basic doc-level metadata
  - Text Processing & Segmentation: clean and split into sections/chunks
  - Indexing: create or upsert index entries for each section
  - On success: set Document status to “completed” and store index references
  - On failure: increment retry counter, apply backoff, set status to “failed” after max retries
- Clients can query Status API to learn progress; Search API uses index to return results
- If the same file is uploaded again (duplicate content/hash), return an idempotent response and relevant metadata without reprocessing

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

- Compatibility tests
  - Easy addition of a new format: add extraction rule stub and verify flow remains intact

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Complexity vs. simplicity in the indexing approach
  - We choose a modular, pluggable indexing design (supporting inverted index or vector-like indexing) to keep the system maintainable for a small team. This allows starting with a simple inverted index (string terms) and incrementally introducing vector-based relevance if needed. The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential future migration work, but gains flexibility to adapt to evolving requirements without a full rewrite.

- Validation: reject unsupported mime types or oversized files with 400 and explicit messages.

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

8. Testing Plan
- Unit tests
  - Validation logic for file type/size
  - Hash/deduplication decision paths
  - Text extraction interfaces with mock backends for PDF/TXT/Markdown
  - Segmentation rules: section boundaries, token/window size
  - Indexing/upsert logic for sections
- Integration tests
  - End-to-end upload flow for each supported format
  - Duplicate upload flow returns existing document
  - Asynchronous processing path with simulated recoverable errors
- End-to-end tests
  - Upload a sample document, poll status to completion, perform a search over inserted sections
  - Validate search results relevance and snippet content
- Performance tests (light)
  - Large document processing (resource usage, backoff behavior)
- Compatibility tests
  - Easy addition of a new format: add extraction rule stub and verify flow remains intact

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Complexity vs. simplicity in the indexing approach
  - We choose a modular, pluggable indexing design (supporting inverted index or vector-like indexing) to keep the system maintainable for a small team. This allows starting with a simple inverted index (string terms) and incrementally introducing vector-based relevance if needed. The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential future migration work, but gains flexibility to adapt to evolving requirements without a full rewrite.

Executive summary
The SE-04 design provides a solid, modular backend architecture for a document-processing and search service supporting PDF, TXT, and Markdown uploads. It emphasizes asynchronous processing, retryable steps, duplicate avoidance via content hashing, and easy extensibility for new formats. The proposal remains implementation-agnostic, which is appropriate for a small team but requires concrete interface definitions, data-store decisions, and clear API error semantics to move toward implementation.

Key recalculations and constraints check
- File formats: Explicitly supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown) are consistently enforced in validation and workflow. No changes needed.
- File validation: Size/type checks are explicit; error handling for invalid inputs is defined (400 with descriptive payloads). No changes needed.
- Deduplication: Content-hash-based dedupe path; duplicates return existing document_id with current status. This is idempotent for identical uploads. A potential edge case (metadata differences with identical content) should be clarified in an ADR, but the current design supports the primary use case.
- Asynchronous processing: Orchestrator with retries and exponential backoff (up to a max, e.g., 5 attempts) is specified. This aligns with resilience goals; ensure backoff policy is codified in the implementation to avoid thundering retries.
- Indexing approach: Start with a pluggable/inverted-index-based design, with vector search as a future enhancement. This balances simplicity now with extensibility later. Do not lock into a single backend; keep an abstraction layer.
- Data model and workflow: Document, Section, Index, Storage, and Status/Events models align with the workflow: ingest, extract, segment, index, search. End-to-end steps map cleanly to services.
- Testing: Coverage spans unit, integration, and end-to-end tests, including duplicate upload scenarios and transient-error handling. Ensure test data includes all three formats and representative edge cases.
- Project structure: Modular components and a shallow, maintainable codebase are provided. This supports a small team and future feature adds.

Grounded conclusions by constraint

1) System overview fidelity
- The architecture meets core objectives: support for PDF/TXT/Markdown, validation, persistence of originals and metadata, text extraction, segmentation, indexing, search, asynchronous processing, retries, and duplicate prevention. It also accommodates growth (new formats, different storage/search backends) with a modular design.

2) Main components viability
- The component decomposition covers all lifecycle stages: ingestion, orchestration, extraction, segmentation, indexing, storage, search, and status/events. This separation supports independent testing and straightforward maintenance.

3) End-to-end workflow correctness
- The end-to-end sequence (upload -> validate hash -> dedupe check -> store original/metadata -> enqueue -> extract -> segment -> index -> status completed or failed with retries) is coherent and implementable. Duplicates are handled idempotently; status can be queried; search uses the index to produce results.

4) REST API design practicality
- Endpoints (/uploads, /uploads/{id}/status, /documents/{id}, /search, /health) cover the required interactions. Consistent response shapes and error payloads are defined. For production, consider expanding with /healthz and /metrics endpoints and possibly versioning.

5) Data model adequacy
- Document, Section, Index, Storage, and Processing Status entities capture necessary attributes to support dedupe, indexing, search, and observability. The separation of Storage and Original File references aligns with durability and retrievability goals.

6) Project structure maintainability
- The proposed src layout (api, services, models, worker) supports incremental development, easy testing, and clear ownership. Interfaces for adapters (extraction, segmentation, indexing) can be defined to enforce consistency.

7) Error handling, retry, and duplicates
- Validation errors -> 400 with explicit messages.
- Dedupe path ensures idempotent behavior on duplicates; consider metadata-difference edge cases in ADRs.
- Retries use exponential backoff; steps are designed to be idempotent to avoid side effects on retries.
- Observability is addressed via per-document status/event records; consider integrating metrics for queue depth and processing latency.

8) Testing strategy sufficiency
- The plan covers unit, integration, and end-to-end testing, including large documents and failure/retry scenarios. For practical adoption, define concrete test datasets, mock adapters for extraction/segmentation/indexing, and CI pipelines with deterministic run orders.

9) Design tradeoff interpretation
- Tradeoff chosen: modular, pluggable indexing (inverted index now, vector search later) to maximize maintainability and incremental enhancement for a small team. Acceptable complexity overhead for the flexibility gained; ensure clean abstraction boundaries to minimize migration pain later.
