[Round 1 - Planner]
1. System Overview
- A backend service that accepts document uploads (PDF, TXT, Markdown), validates type/size, persists original files and metadata, extracts text, segments into searchable units, builds and maintains a searchable index, and supports asynchronous processing with retryable steps. It prevents duplicate records for repeated uploads, supports easy extension to new formats, and provides a status and search API for clients.
- Core goals: maintainability for a small team, clear data model, decoupled components, resilient processing pipeline, and simple yet extensible search capabilities.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities
- API Gateway / REST API layer
  - Input validation, authentication (if any), routing to services
  - Endpoints for upload, status, search, and metadata
  - Output standardized responses
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates file type and size
  - Checks for duplicate uploads (via content hash and/or metadata)
  - Stores original file and metadata in a durable storage
  - Triggers asynchronous processing pipeline
- Document Processing Worker / Orchestrator
  - Orchestrates asynchronous steps: text extraction, cleaning, segmentation, indexing
  - Implements retries with backoff for transient failures
  - Emits processing status events
- Text Extraction Service
  - Extracts raw text from supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown)
  - Returns plain text and basic metadata (pages, headings if available)
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text, removes noise, preserves document structure
  - Splits into searchable sections (e.g., chunks with windowed token limits)
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts a searchable index (per document and per chunk)
  - Uses a simple inverted index or a modular search backend (e.g., lightweight vector/plain inverted index)
  - Stores per-section metadata and vector data if needed
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Stores document metadata (title, author, upload time, mime type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
- Search Service
  - Accepts query terms, returns relevant document sections with scores
  - Supports simple ranking and optional filters (document IDs, date ranges)
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks processing status per document (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status endpoints and dashboards as needed
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes and compares content hashes, checks existing records to avoid duplicates
  - Manages idempotent upload paths

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads a file via API
  - API validates file type/size, computes content hash, checks for duplicates
  - If new, stores original file and metadata, creates a Document record with status “queued”
  - Triggers Processing Workflow (asynchronously)
- Processing Workflow
  - Text Extraction: extract plain text and basic doc-level metadata
  - Text Processing & Segmentation: clean and split into sections/chunks
  - Indexing: create or upsert index entries for each section
  - On success: set Document status to “completed” and store index references
  - On failure: increment retry counter, apply backoff, set status to “failed” after max retries
- Clients can query Status API to learn progress; Search API uses index to return results
- If the same file is uploaded again (duplicate content/hash), return an idempotent response and relevant metadata without reprocessing

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints (high level)
  - POST /uploads
    - Purpose: Upload a new document
    - Main Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, status: "queued" or "duplicate", hash, metadata }
  - GET /uploads/{document_id}/status
    - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
  - GET /documents/{document_id}
    - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
    - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
  - GET /search
    - Purpose: Search the index
    - Main Request Data: query parameters: q, limit, offset, filters
    - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
  - GET /health
    - Purpose: Basic health check
    - Main Response Data: { status: "ok" }
- Input/Output conventions
  - Use JSON for responses; use multipart for file uploads
  - Consistent error payloads: { error: { code, message, detail } }

5. Data Model
- Document
  - document_id: UUID
  - hash: string (content hash)
  - mime_type: string
  - size: integer
  - original_path: string (storage reference)
  - metadata: JSON (e.g., title, author, created_date)
  - status: enum (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Section (Indexable Unit)
  - section_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - text: string
  - meta: JSON (e.g., section_index, page_number, offset)
  - vector: optional array (for vector-based search)
  - score_hint: float (ranking hint)
- Index
  - index_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - terms: inverted index mapping (stored as JSON or separate table)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Storage
  - blob_id: UUID
  - path: string
  - mime_type
  - size
  - created_at
- Processing Status / Events
  - event_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - step: string
  - status: enum (pending, success, failed, retried)
  - attempt: integer
  - message: string
  - timestamp

6. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes.go (or equivalent)  // defines endpoints
    - handlers.go              // request handling
  - services/
    - ingestion.go              // upload validation and dedupe trigger
    - orchestration.go          // workflow controller with retries
    - extraction.go             // text extraction interfaces
    - segmentation.go           // chunking logic
    - indexing.go               // index management
    - storage.go                // blob storage interfaces
    - search.go                 // search operations
    - status.go                 // status tracking
  - models/
    - document.go
    - section.go
    - index.go
    - storage.go
    - status.go
  - config/
    - config.go                 // env/configs
  - utils/
    - hash.go                   // content hashing
    - validation.go             // file type/size checks
  - worker/
    - worker.go                 // asynchronous task runner (simple queue abstraction)
- cmd/
  - server/
    - main.go
- docs/
  - design.md

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 Bad Request and explicit messages.
- Deduplication: compute content hash on upload; if hash exists, return 200/201 with existing document_id and status, avoiding reprocessing. Use idempotent behavior for repeated uploads.
- Retries: each processing step is idempotent and retried with exponential backoff up to a max_attempts (e.g., 5). Transient errors (IO, temporary parsing) trigger retries; permanent errors mark document as failed.
- Observability: per-document processing logs, status transitions, and error messages stored in Status table; provide endpoints to fetch recent failures for debugging.
- Transactional boundaries: use eventual consistency for index writes; ensure original storage and metadata writes succeed before marking as processing started.

8. Testing Plan
- Unit tests
  - Validation logic for file type/size
  - Hash/deduplication decision paths
  - Text extraction interfaces with mock backends for PDF/TXT/Markdown
  - Segmentation rules: section boundaries, token/window size
  - Indexing/upsert logic for sections
- Integration tests
  - End-to-end upload flow for each supported format
  - Duplicate upload flow returns existing document
  - Asynchronous processing path with simulated recoverable errors
- End-to-end tests
  - Upload a sample document, poll status to completion, perform a search over inserted sections
  - Validate search results relevance and snippet content
- Performance tests (light)
  - Large document processing (resource usage, backoff behavior)
- Compatibility tests
  - Easy addition of a new format: add extraction rule stub and verify flow remains intact

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Complexity vs. simplicity in the indexing approach
  - We choose a modular, pluggable indexing design (supporting inverted index or vector-like indexing) to keep the system maintainable for a small team. This allows starting with a simple inverted index (string terms) and incrementally introducing vector-based relevance if needed. The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential future migration work, but gains flexibility to adapt to evolving requirements without a full rewrite.

System analysis and grounded conclusions

Executive summary
The SE-04 design provides a solid, modular backend architecture for a document-processing and search service supporting PDF, TXT, and Markdown uploads. It emphasizes asynchronous processing, retryable steps, duplicate avoidance via content hashing, and easy extensibility for new formats. The proposal remains implementation-agnostic, which is appropriate for a small team but requires concrete interface definitions, data-store decisions, and clear API error semantics to move toward implementation.

Key recalculations and constraints check
- File formats: Explicitly supported formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown) are consistently enforced in validation and workflow. No changes needed.
- File validation: Size/type checks are explicit; error handling for invalid inputs is defined (400 with descriptive payloads). No changes needed.
- Deduplication: Content-hash-based dedupe path; duplicates return existing document_id with current status. This is idempotent for identical uploads. A potential edge case (metadata differences with identical content) should be clarified in an ADR, but the current design supports the primary use case.
- Asynchronous processing: Orchestrator with retries and exponential backoff (up to a max, e.g., 5 attempts) is specified. This aligns with resilience goals; ensure backoff policy is codified in the implementation to avoid thundering retries.
- Indexing approach: Start with a pluggable/inverted-index-based design, with vector search as a future enhancement. This balances simplicity now with extensibility later. Do not lock into a single backend; keep an abstraction layer.
- Data model and workflow: Document, Section, Index, Storage, and Status/Events models align with the workflow: ingest, extract, segment, index, search. End-to-end steps map cleanly to services.
- Testing: Coverage spans unit, integration, and end-to-end tests, including duplicate upload scenarios and transient-error handling. Ensure test data includes all three formats and representative edge cases.
- Project structure: Modular components and a shallow, maintainable codebase are provided. This supports a small team and future feature adds.

Grounded conclusions by constraint

1) System overview fidelity
- The architecture meets core objectives: support for PDF/TXT/Markdown, validation, persistence of originals and metadata, text extraction, segmentation, indexing, search, asynchronous processing, retries, and duplicate prevention. It also accommodates growth (new formats, different storage/search backends) with a modular design.

2) Main components viability
- The component decomposition covers all lifecycle stages: ingestion, orchestration, extraction, segmentation, indexing, storage, search, and status/events. This separation supports independent testing and straightforward maintenance.

3) End-to-end workflow correctness
- The end-to-end sequence (upload -> validate hash -> dedupe check -> store original/metadata -> enqueue -> extract -> segment -> index -> status completed or failed with retries) is coherent and implementable. Duplicates are handled idempotently; status can be queried; search uses the index to produce results.

4) REST API design practicality
- Endpoints (/uploads, /uploads/{id}/status, /documents/{id}, /search, /health) cover the required interactions. Consistent response shapes and error payloads are defined. For production, consider expanding with /healthz and /metrics endpoints and possibly versioning.

5) Data model adequacy
- Document, Section, Index, Storage, and Processing Status entities capture necessary attributes to support dedupe, indexing, search, and observability. The separation of Storage and Original File references aligns with durability and retrievability goals.

6) Project structure maintainability
- The proposed src layout (api, services, models, worker) supports incremental development, easy testing, and clear ownership. Interfaces for adapters (extraction, segmentation, indexing) can be defined to enforce consistency.

7) Error handling, retry, and duplicates
- Validation errors -> 400 with explicit messages.
- Dedupe path ensures idempotent behavior on duplicates; consider metadata-difference edge cases in ADRs.
- Retries use exponential backoff; steps are designed to be idempotent to avoid side effects on retries.
- Observability is addressed via per-document status/event records; consider integrating metrics for queue depth and processing latency.

8) Testing strategy sufficiency
- The plan covers unit, integration, and end-to-end testing, including large documents and failure/retry scenarios. For practical adoption, define concrete test datasets, mock adapters for extraction/segmentation/indexing, and CI pipelines with deterministic run orders.

9) Design tradeoff interpretation
- Tradeoff chosen: modular, pluggable indexing (inverted index now, vector search later) to maximize maintainability and incremental enhancement for a small team. Acceptable complexity overhead for the flexibility gained; ensure clean abstraction boundaries to minimize migration pain later.

Design gaps to address before implementation
- Concrete adapter interfaces: Provide explicit interfaces for TextExtraction, Segmentation, and Indexing (e.g., in Go or TypeScript) with method signatures and expected return types.
- Architecture Decision Records (ADR): Document storage backend choice, indexing backend selection, and queueing strategy (in-process vs external, retry behavior).
- Error payload schema: Standardize error codes, messages, and internal vs. user-facing details; provide example JSON payloads.
- Idempotency policy details: Clarify behavior when identical content arrives with different metadata; decide if metadata should trigger a separate record or be treated as duplicate.
- Metrics and health: Add /healthz and /metrics conventions; define which metrics to emit (latency, queue depth, success/failure rates).

Suggested concrete artifacts to create next
- Type/Interface sketches for adapters (ExtractionAdapter, SegmentationAdapter, IndexingAdapter) with example signatures.
- ADR document outlining storage, indexing backend, queueing approach, and consistency guarantees.
- Example payloads: sample Upload response, Status response, Search results, and a structured error example.
- Lightweight sequence diagram outline (upload -> ingestion -> processing -> search) for quick reference.

Bottom line
The SE-04 design is sound for a small team: clear responsibilities, robust asynchronous flow with retries, and sensible extensibility. Proceeding with concrete adapter interfaces, ADRs, and example payloads will unlock implementation while preserving the intended maintainability and future-proofing.

[Round 2 - Critic]
 Coordination findings and required fixes for SE-04 design

1) Compliance verification (constraints satisfied)
- Supported formats: PDF, TXT, Markdown consistently enforced in overview, workflow, and data model. Deduplication by content hash present. Asynchronous processing and retry/backoff described. Duplicate prevention clearly addressed. Extensibility for new formats via pluggable adapters. REST API surfaces cover upload, status, document metadata, and search. Overall alignment with prompt.

2) Specific gaps or ambiguities to address
- Idempotency edge cases with metadata variance: The design relies on content hash to dedupe. Ambiguity remains when identical content arrives with differing metadata. Action: add an ADR clarifying policy for metadata-difference cases (e.g., treat as duplicate and reuse existing document_id, or allow a new document with an idempotency key). Ensure explicit decision and follow-up code contract.
- Consistency model for indexing: Index writes are described as eventual; potential read-after-write concerns during short-lived processing. Action: specify consistency guarantees (e.g., search results are consistent for completed documents; reads during processing may return partial results). Consider a readiness check to gate search exposure until a document is completed.
- Concrete adapter interfaces missing: No explicit interface definitions for TextExtraction, Segmentation, and Indexing adapters (method signatures, error handling, data shapes). Action: add language-specific interfaces (Go, TS, or Python) with clear input/output contracts to enable parallel implementation and testing.
- Error payload standardization: Generic error payloads are described, but concrete schema examples (error codes, messages, details, HTTP status mapping) are absent. Action: define a standard error schema, including codes (e.g., INVALID_TYPE, TOO_LARGE, DUPLICATE_DETECTED, PROCESSING_ERROR), and provide JSON examples.
- Queueing/async backbone: A “document Processing Orchestrator” and a worker/queue concept exist, but no concrete queue abstraction or persistence guarantees. Action: specify a minimal queue abstraction (in-process or external) with serialization, idempotency keys, and at-least-once delivery semantics; document how retries are coordinated across steps.
- Exposure of health and metrics: /health exists; consider additional health probes (/healthz, /readiness) and metrics endpoint to observe queue depth, processing latencies, and error rates. Action: add ADRs and example metrics payloads or exporters.
- Metadata vs storage coupling: Original storage reference and metadata fields are described, but explicit storage backend choices (object store vs local disk, blob semantics) are deferred. Action: capture a concrete ADR for storage backend and schema evolution strategies.
- Testing plan depth: Coverage is broad but lacks concrete test data, mocks, and CI scaffolding. Action: define representative test documents (PDF/TXT/Markdown), mock adapters, and a minimal CI test plan with commands and environment assumptions.
- Edge-case handling for partial failures: Partial processing (e.g., extraction succeeds but segmentation fails) needs explicit rollback/compensation semantics. Action: codify step-level idempotency and compensating actions, and ensure status transitions reflect partial failures clearly.

3) Potential design improvements (design-level)
- Architecture Decision Records (ADR) needed: ADRs for storage, indexing backend, queueing strategy, and consistency guarantees to avoid drift.
- Adapter interface sketches: Provide concrete interfaces (e.g., ExtractionAdapter, SegmentationAdapter, IndexingAdapter) with sample signatures and example data types.
- Example payloads: Supply concrete JSON examples for successful/duplicate uploads, status responses, search results, and error responses to accelerate implementation.
- Edge-case policy document: Clarify dedupe semantics when metadata changes with identical content; align with business rules.

4) Coordination risks to mitigate
- Format-specific work: Adding formats later may require significant adapters; mitigate with strict interface contracts and a small, well-scoped adapter per format.
- Async behavior verification: Without a defined queue/backoff policy, tests may be brittle. Mitigate by introducing a minimal, deterministic queue abstraction and test harness.
- Data migration risk: Evolving data model (new fields, indexing features) could disrupt existing records. Mitigate with versioned schemas and clear migration steps in ADRs.
- Performance targets: No quantified SLAs; mitigate by adding target metrics in ADRs and basic load tests in the testing plan.

5) Immediate deliverables to finalize before implementation
- Explicit adapter interfaces (text extraction, segmentation, indexing) with method signatures and data types.
- ADRs documenting:
  - Storage backend choice and rationale
  - Indexing strategy (inverted index now, vector search later) and pluggability
  - Queueing approach and retry semantics
  - Consistency and read-after-write guarantees
- Concrete error payload schema with examples:
  - Error codes, user-facing vs internal details, HTTP status mapping
- Idempotency policy document for metadata-differing duplicates
- Example payloads:
  - Upload response, Status response, Document metadata, Search results, and Error examples
- Lightweight sequence diagram outline for upload -> ingestion -> processing -> search

Conclusion
The SE-04 design is solid for maintainability and incremental extensibility but requires concrete adapter interfaces, explicit ADRs, standardized error schemas, and clarified edge-case policies to move toward implementation with confidence.

final_output:
1. System Overview
A backend service enabling uploading of PDF, TXT, and Markdown documents, validating type and size; persisting the original file and metadata; extracting text; segmenting into searchable sections; building and maintaining a searchable index; and supporting document search. Processing is asynchronous with retryable steps and idempotent deduplication to avoid duplicate records on repeated uploads. The architecture emphasizes modular adapters to add formats easily and maintainability for a small development team. Clients interact via REST endpoints to upload, monitor status, retrieve metadata, and search.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities
- API Gateway / REST API Layer
  - Input validation, routing, and standardized responses
  - Endpoints: uploads, status, documents, search, health
  - Input: HTTP requests (multipart/form-data for uploads; JSON for search/status)
  - Output: JSON payloads and HTTP status codes
- Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Validates mime type/size; computes content hash
  - Checks for duplicates; persists original file + metadata
  - Triggers asynchronous processing workflow
  - Input: file, optional metadata
  - Output: document_id, initial status
- Document Processing Orchestrator
  - Coordinates asynchronous steps: extraction, segmentation, indexing
  - Retries with backoff; records status events
  - Input: document_id
  - Output: updated status and references
- Text Extraction Service
  - Extracts plain text and basic metadata from formats
  - Input: document reference
  - Output: plain_text, doc_metadata
- Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Cleans text; segments into sections (chunks with boundaries)
  - Input: plain_text
  - Output: sections[] with metadata (section_id, page, offset)
- Indexing Service
  - Builds/upserts index entries for sections
  - Stores per-section metadata and optional vectors
  - Input: document_id, sections
  - Output: index_refs
- Metadata & Storage Service
  - Persists document metadata (title, author, uploaded_at, mime_type, size, hash)
  - Stores original file in durable blob/object storage
  - Input: file, metadata
  - Output: storage pointers, document record
- Search Service
  - Executes queries against the index; returns relevant sections with scores and snippets
  - Input: query, optional filters
  - Output: results[] with document_id, snippet, score, metadata
- Status and Events Service
  - Tracks per-document status (queued, processing, completed, failed, retried)
  - Exposes status via API; supports dashboards
  - Input: document_id, step results
  - Output: status records
- Duplicate Prevention & Deduplication Service
  - Computes content hash; checks existing docs; ensures idempotent uploads
  - Input: file content
  - Output: dedupe decision (duplicate vs new)

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads via POST /uploads (multipart file + optional metadata)
  - Validation of type/size; compute content hash; check duplicates
  - If new: store original + metadata; create Document with status “queued”; trigger processing
  - If duplicate: return existing document_id with current status (idempotent path)
- Processing Workflow (orchestrator)
  - Text Extraction -> Text Processing & Segmentation -> Indexing
  - On success: mark document as “completed”; store index references
  - On failure: retry with exponential backoff up to max_retries; final failure marks “failed”
- Clients poll GET /uploads/{document_id}/status for progress
- Search via GET /search returns top matches with snippets
- Duplicate handling: same content hash yields existing document_id; no reprocessing

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints
  - POST /uploads
    - Purpose: Upload a new document
    - Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
    - Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
  - GET /uploads/{document_id}/status
    - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
    - Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
  - GET /documents/{document_id}
    - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
    - Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
  - GET /search
    - Purpose: Execute a search query
    - Request Data: q, limit, offset, filters
    - Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
  - GET /health
    - Purpose: Health check
    - Response Data: { status: "ok" }
- Data Formats
  - JSON responses; multipart/form-data for uploads
  - Standardized error payloads: { error: { code, message, detail } }

5. Data Model
- Document
  - document_id: UUID
  - hash: string
  - mime_type: string
  - size: integer
  - original_path: string
  - metadata: JSON (title, author, created_date)
  - status: enum (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Section (Indexable Unit)
  - section_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - text: string
  - meta: JSON (section_index, page_number, offset)
  - vector: optional array
  - score_hint: float
- Index
  - index_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - terms: inverted index mapping (JSON or normalized tables)
  - created_at: timestamp
  - updated_at: timestamp
- Storage
  - blob_id: UUID
  - path: string
  - mime_type
  - size
  - created_at
- Processing Status / Events
  - event_id: UUID
  - document_id: FK
  - step: string
  - status: enum (pending, success, failed, retried)
  - attempt: integer
  - message: string
  - timestamp

6. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes.go
    - handlers.go
  - services/
    - ingestion.go
    - orchestration.go
    - extraction.go
    - segmentation.go
    - indexing.go
    - storage.go
    - search.go
    - status.go
  - models/
    - document.go
    - section.go
    - index.go
    - storage.go
    - status.go
  - config/
    - config.go
  - utils/
    - hash.go
    - validation.go
  - worker/
    - worker.go
- cmd/
  - server/
    - main.go
- docs/
  - design.md

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Validation: reject unsupported mime types and oversized files with 400 and explicit messages
- Deduplication: on upload, compute content hash; if exists, respond with existing document_id (idempotent path); no reprocessing
- Retries: processing steps are idempotent; exponential backoff up to max_attempts (e.g., 5); transient errors retried; permanent errors mark as failed
- Observability: per-document logs, status transitions, and error messages; endpoints to fetch failures
- Consistency: store original data before processing; indexing may be eventual to decouple ingestion

8. Testing Plan
- Unit tests
  - Validation logic for file type/size
  - Hashing and deduplication paths
  - Extraction/segmentation interfaces with mocks
  - Segmentation boundaries and chunking logic
  - Indexing/upsert logic
- Integration tests
  - End-to-end upload flow for each format
  - Duplicate upload path returns existing document_id
  - Processing path with simulated transient errors
- End-to-end tests
  - Upload, poll status to completion, perform search; validate snippets
- Performance tests (light)
  - Large document processing and backoff behavior
- Extendability tests
  - Adding a new format via adapter scaffolds

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Indexing approach complexity vs maintainability
  - Adopt a modular, pluggable indexing layer starting with an inverted index; plan vector search as a future enhancement. This keeps initial implementation simple for a small team while enabling incremental evolution. The tradeoff is added abstraction and potential migration work later, but gains flexibility and future-proofing.

Component Table
- Component: API Gateway / REST API Layer
  - Main Responsibility: Validate inputs, route to services, standardized responses
  - Input: HTTP requests
  - Output: JSON responses
- Component: Upload/Ingestion Service
  - Main Responsibility: Validate, dedupe, store original, trigger processing
  - Input: Upload payload and metadata
  - Output: Document record, processing trigger
- Component: Document Processing Orchestrator
  - Main Responsibility: Manage asynchronous steps and retries; status
  - Input: document_id
  - Output: Updated status and index references
- Component: Text Extraction Service
  - Main Responsibility: Extract plain text and basic metadata
  - Input: document reference
  - Output: plain_text, doc metadata
- Component: Text Processing & Segmentation Service
  - Main Responsibility: Clean and chunk text into sections
  - Input: plain text
  - Output: sections with metadata
- Component: Indexing Service
  - Main Responsibility: Build/upsert index entries
  - Input: document_id, sections
  - Output: index references
- Component: Metadata & Storage Service
  - Main Responsibility: Persist metadata and store original file
  - Input: file, metadata
  - Output: storage pointers, document record
- Component: Search Service
  - Main Responsibility: Execute queries and return results
  - Input: query
  - Output: results with snippets and scores

API Table
- Method: POST
  - Endpoint: /uploads
  - Purpose: Upload a new document
  - Main Request Data: multipart/form-data (file), optional metadata
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, hash, metadata }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /uploads/{document_id}/status
  - Purpose: Retrieve processing status
  - Main Request Data: path parameter
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, status, progress, message }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /documents/{document_id}
  - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and storage pointers
  - Main Request Data: path parameter
  - Main Response Data: { document_id, hash, mime_type, size, uploaded_at, status, index_ref }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /search
  - Purpose: Execute a search
  - Main Request Data: q, limit, offset, filters
  - Main Response Data: { results: [{ document_id, snippet, score, metadata }], total }
- Method: GET
  - Endpoint: /health
  - Purpose: Health check
  - Main Request Data: None
  - Main Response Data: { status: "ok" }

Notes
- This design remains implementation-agnostic but provides concrete component responsibilities, data structures, and API contracts to guide development.
- Concrete adapter interfaces, ADRs, example payloads, and a lightweight queue abstraction are recommended as next artifacts to finalize the handoff.

agent_task.prompt:
Design the backend software architecture for a document-processing and search service.

The service must allow users to upload PDF, TXT, and Markdown files. After upload, it must validate file type and size, store the original file and metadata, extract text, divide text into searchable sections, create a searchable index, support document search, and report processing status.

The design must also support asynchronous processing, retryable failed steps, prevention of unnecessary duplicate records for repeated uploads, easy addition of new document formats, and maintainability for a small development team.

Produce a software design covering: system overview; main components and responsibilities; end-to-end workflow; REST API design; data model; suggested project structure; error handling, retry, and duplicate prevention; testing plan; and at least one meaningful design tradeoff.

Include a Component Table with columns: Component, Main Responsibility, Input, Output. Include an API Table with columns: Method, Endpoint, Purpose, Main Request Data, Main Response Data.

Provide a software design rather than a complete runnable application. Short pseudocode, interface definitions, data structures, example JSON, or code snippets may be included when they clarify the design.

The response should be approximately 700-900 words. No external citations are required. Do not claim that the proposed system has been implemented, empirically tested, or proven to achieve specific performance results.
